  function setTheme(theme) {
    document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', theme)

    const schemes = {
      default: 'light dark',
      light: 'light',
      dark: 'dark',
    }

    const meta = document.getElementById('themeMeta')

    if (meta) {
      meta.setAttribute('content', schemes[theme] ?? schemes['default'])
    } else {
      const meta = document.createElement('meta')
      meta.name = 'color-scheme'
      meta.id = 'themeMeta'
      meta.content = schemes[theme] ?? schemes['default']

      document.head.insertAdjacentElement('afterbegin', meta)
    }
  }

  window.addEventListener('storage', () => {
    setTheme(localStorage.getItem('theme'))
  })

  if (['light', 'dark'].includes(localStorage.getItem('theme'))) {
    setTheme(localStorage.getItem('theme'))
  } else {
    setTheme('default')
  }

class="component-GuideContent" data-rw-cid-73lx6ffk=""><header data-rw-cid-73lx6ffk=""><h2 data-rw-cid-73lx6ffk="">Building a tool-calling agent with Kimi K2.6</h2><p class="description" data-rw-cid-73lx6ffk="">How to give Kimi K2.6 access to your own functions through Runware's OpenAI-compatible endpoint: defining tools, running the call loop, making parallel tool calls, and controlling tool selection.</p></header><div class="layout-body" data-rw-cid-73lx6ffk=""><div class="component-Prose"><section id="introduction"><h3 class="component-H3" id="introduction" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy=""><a href="#introduction" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy="">Introduction</a></h3><p>Tool calling lets Kimi K2.6 pause mid-response to call functions you define, read what they return, and finish its answer with that data in hand. You describe each function as a JSON schema, the model decides <strong>when to call it and with what arguments</strong>, and your code runs the function and passes the result back. Kimi K2.6 is built for this style of work: it handles <strong>multi-step, long-horizon tasks</strong> and chains several tool calls together before it commits to an answer.</p><p>Give Kimi K2.6 one instruction and four read/write tools (service health, logs, deploys, and a pager), and it works the problem end to end. From that single request it ran <strong>four tool calls across three rounds</strong>: it pulled health, deploys, and logs together, paged the on-call engineer based on what it found, then wrote up the incident, shown in full under <a href="#the-agent-loop" class="component-A" data-rw-cid-suiksw2l="">The agent loop</a> below.</p><p>The model never saw your services directly. It only saw the tools you described and the JSON they returned. This guide builds that assistant from a single call up to the full loop.</p></section>
<section id="connecting-to-the-endpoint"><h3 class="component-H3" id="connecting-to-the-endpoint" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy=""><a href="#connecting-to-the-endpoint" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy="">Connecting to the endpoint</a></h3><p>Tool calling runs over Runware's <a href="/docs/platform/openai" class="component-A" data-rw-cid-suiksw2l=""><span class="icon" data-rw-cid-suiksw2l=""><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" aria-hidden="true" data-rw-cid-suiksw2l="true" class="lucide lucide-plug"><path d="M12 22v-5"></path><path d="M15 8V2"></path><path d="M17 8a1 1 0 0 1 1 1v4a4 4 0 0 1-4 4h-4a4 4 0 0 1-4-4V9a1 1 0 0 1 1-1z"></path><path d="M9 8V2"></path></svg></span>OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions endpoint</a> at <code class="component-Code">https://api.runware.ai/v1</code>. Point any OpenAI client at that base URL with your Runware API key, and set the model to <code class="component-Code">moonshotai-kimi-k2-6</code>. The request and response follow the <strong>standard OpenAI tool-calling format</strong>, so existing agent code and frameworks work unchanged.</p><div class="component-Tabs"><input type="radio" name="26a01ad0-8bd9-4483-afec-cf38aeab1902" id="ea32c492-4c90-4a74-9e24-1bee4f375f13" checked=""><label for="ea32c492-4c90-4a74-9e24-1bee4f375f13">Python</label><div class="component-Tab content-ea32c492-4c90-4a74-9e24-1bee4f375f13" data-id="python"><div class="component-Snippet"><figure><div class="code"><pre class="rw rw-themes min-light min-dark" style="--rw-light:#24292eff;--rw-dark:#b392f0;--rw-light-bg:#ffffff;--rw-dark-bg:#1f1f1f" tabindex="0"><code class="language-python"><span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-red-text);--rw-dark:var(--color-red-text)">from</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> openai </span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-red-text);--rw-dark:var(--color-red-text)">import</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> OpenAI</span></span>

Final answer</label><div class="component-Tab content-4f05bba9-40af-462e-9c13-d6b1403a2183" data-id="4-final-answer"><div class="component-Snippet"><figure><div class="code"><pre class="rw rw-themes min-light min-dark" style="--rw-light:#24292eff;--rw-dark:#b392f0;--rw-light-bg:#ffffff;--rw-dark-bg:#1f1f1f" tabindex="0"><code class="language-markdown"><span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0">The payments service is currently </span><span style="--rw-light:#6F42C1;--rw-light-font-weight:bold;--rw-dark:#FF7A84;--rw-dark-font-weight:bold">**healthy**</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> with an error rate of</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:#6F42C1;--rw-light-font-weight:bold;--rw-dark:#FF7A84;--rw-dark-font-weight:bold">**0.2%**</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> (0.002) and a p95 latency of </span><span style="--rw-light:#6F42C1;--rw-light-font-weight:bold;--rw-dark:#FF7A84;--rw-dark-font-weight:bold">**180 ms**</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0">. No immediate action is required.</span></span></code></pre><button class="component-SnippetCopyButton" aria-label="Copy code"><span class="icon"></span><span class="label"></span></button></div></figure></div></div></div><p>Three details carry the protocol. When <code class="component-Code">finish_reason</code> is <code class="component-Code">tool_calls</code>, the assistant message has <strong>no text to show the user yet</strong>: its <code class="component-Code">content</code> is null and the call lives in the <code class="component-Code">tool_calls</code> array. The <code class="component-Code">arguments</code> field is a <strong>JSON-encoded string, not an object</strong>, so you parse it before use. Your reply is a <code class="component-Code">tool</code> message whose <code class="component-Code">tool_call_id</code> must match the call's <code class="component-Code">id</code>, which is how the model pairs your result with the request it made. Append both the assistant message and your tool message to the history, send it back, and the model answers with <code class="component-Code">finish_reason: "stop"</code>.</p></section>
<section id="the-agent-loop"><h3 class="component-H3" id="the-agent-loop" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy=""><a href="#the-agent-loop" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy="">The agent loop</a></h3><p>One call answers one question. Real tasks need a loop, because the model often calls more tools after seeing the first results. The rule is simple: send the conversation, append whatever comes back, and <strong>repeat until the model returns no more tool calls</strong>.</p><div class="component-Tabs"><input type="radio" name="098ff1d0-1623-4b0b-85e3-88be1c3d8518" id="ca5609bf-b521-4325-9633-140c2f99cf60" checked=""><label for="ca5609bf-b521-4325-9633-140c2f99cf60">Python</label><div class="component-Tab content-ca5609bf-b521-4325-9633-140c2f99cf60" data-id="python"><div class="component-Snippet"><figure><div class="code"><pre class="rw rw-themes min-light min-dark" style="--rw-light:#24292eff;--rw-dark:#b392f0;--rw-light-bg:#ffffff;--rw-dark-bg:#1f1f1f" tabindex="0"><code class="language-python"><span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-red-text);--rw-dark:var(--color-red-text)">import</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> json</span></span>

error rate is <strong>18.2%</strong> and p95 latency is <strong>4,200 ms</strong>.</p><p><strong>Root cause:</strong> Deploy <strong>v2.4.1</strong> went live at 13:30. Logs show a <strong>NullPointerException in <code class="component-Code">CheckoutController.applyPromo</code></strong> starting at 13:42, tied directly to the new release.</p><p><strong>Action taken:</strong> Paged the checkout on-call engineer and opened <strong>INC-4827</strong>.</p><p><strong>Recommended immediate next step:</strong> Roll back checkout to <strong>v2.4.0</strong> to stop the bleeding while the team investigates the <code class="component-Code">applyPromo</code> bug.</p></div></div></div></div></div><p><strong>Round 2 is the step a single call can't reach.</strong> The model read the three results, concluded the <code class="component-Code">v2.4.1</code> deploy was the cause, and chose a new action based on them: paging the team. A round that ends in <code class="component-Code">tool_calls</code> sends the loop around again, and the final <code class="component-Code">stop</code> is the model deciding it has enough to answer.</p><div class="component-Callout warning"><div class="body"><p>Always bound the loop. A model can call tools for several rounds, and a bug in a tool (or a tool that keeps failing) can keep it looping. Cap the number of iterations and stop with a clear error when you hit the cap, rather than relying on the model to always finish on its own.</p></div></div></section>

<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">}</span></span></code></pre><button class="component-SnippetCopyButton" aria-label="Copy code"><span class="icon"></span><span class="label"></span></button></div></figure></div><p>The loop above already handles this: it iterates every entry in <code class="component-Code">tool_calls</code> and appends one <code class="component-Code">tool</code> message per call. Because each result is matched back by its own <code class="component-Code">tool_call_id</code>, <strong>you can run independent calls concurrently</strong> and append the results in any order. Once all three came back, the model answered in one line:</p><div class="component-Snippet"><figure><div class="code"><pre class="rw rw-themes min-light min-dark" style="--rw-light:#24292eff;--rw-dark:#b392f0;--rw-light-bg:#ffffff;--rw-dark-bg:#1f1f1f" tabindex="0"><code class="language-markdown"><span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:#6F42C1;--rw-light-font-weight:bold;--rw-dark:#FF7A84;--rw-dark-font-weight:bold">**Checkout**</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> is degraded (18.2% error rate, 4.2s p95 latency); </span><span style="--rw-light:#6F42C1;--rw-light-font-weight:bold;--rw-dark:#FF7A84;--rw-dark-font-weight:bold">**payments**</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> and </span><span style="--rw-light:#6F42C1;--rw-light-font-weight:bold;--rw-dark:#FF7A84;--rw-dark-font-weight:bold">**search**</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> are healthy.</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:#6F42C1;--rw-light-font-weight:bold;--rw-dark:#FF7A84;--rw-dark-font-weight:bold">**Next action:**</span><span style="--rw-light:#24292EFF;--rw-dark:#B392F0"> Investigate checkout immediately—roll back the most recent checkout deploy if one occurred within the latency/error-rate regression window.</span></span></code></pre><button class="component-SnippetCopyButton" aria-label="Copy code"><span class="icon"></span><span class="label"></span></button></div></figure></div></section>
<section id="controlling-tool-selection"><h3 class="component-H3" id="controlling-tool-selection" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy=""><a href="#controlling-tool-selection" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy="">Controlling tool selection</a></h3><p>The <code class="component-Code">tool_choice</code> parameter decides how freely the model reaches for tools. The default, <code class="component-Code">auto</code>, is what every example above uses.</p><div class="component-Table"><table><thead><tr><th class="align-left" style="" data-rw-cid-kzucm6xs="">Value</th><th class="align-left" style="" data-rw-cid-kzucm6xs="">Behavior</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><code class="component-Code">"auto"</code></td><td>The model decides whether to call a tool. The default, and the right choice for most assistants.</td></tr><tr><td><code class="component-Code">"none"</code></td><td>The model answers with text and calls no tools, even if some are defined.</td></tr><tr><td><code class="component-Code">"required"</code></td><td>The model must call at least one tool before it can answer with text.</td></tr><tr><td><code class="component-Code">{ "type": "function", "function": { "name": "..." } }</code></td><td>The model must call the named function.</td></tr></tbody></table></div><p>Naming a function is useful when an action is already decided and you only need the model to <strong>fill in the arguments</strong>. Forcing <code class="component-Code">page_engineer</code> on a paging request guarantees the page fires:</p><div class="component-Snippet"><figure><div class="code"><pre class="rw rw-themes min-light min-dark" style="--rw-light:#24292eff;--rw-dark:#b392f0;--rw-light-bg:#ffffff;--rw-dark-bg:#1f1f1f" tabindex="0"><code class="language-json"><span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-prominent);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-prominent)">  "model"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">:</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-green-text);--rw-dark:var(--color-green-text)"> "moonshotai-kimi-k2-6"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-prominent);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-prominent)">  "messages"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">:</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)"> [ ... ]</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-prominent);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-prominent)">  "tools"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">:</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)"> [ ... ]</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-prominent);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-prominent)">  "tool_choice"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">:</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)"> { </span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-prominent);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-prominent)">"type"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">:</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-green-text);--rw-dark:var(--color-green-text)"> "function"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">,</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-prominent);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-prominent)"> "function"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">:</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)"> { </span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-prominent);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-prominent)">"name"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">:</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-green-text);--rw-dark:var(--color-green-text)"> "page_engineer"</span><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)"> } }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="--rw-light:var(--color-text-subtle);--rw-dark:var(--color-text-subtle)">}</span></span></code></pre><button class="component-SnippetCopyButton" aria-label="Copy code"><span class="icon"></span><span class="label"></span></button></div></figure></div><div class="component-Callout info"><div class="body"><p>Naming a function guarantees Kimi calls it, but the model may still issue other tool calls it judges necessary in the same turn. In the paging run above, Kimi paged as instructed and also called <code class="component-Code">get_service_health</code> and <code class="component-Code">get_deploys</code> to fill in the incident summary. If you need exactly one call and nothing else, expose only that one tool on the request.</p></div></div></section>
<section id="designing-tools-kimi-can-use-well"><h3 class="component-H3" id="designing-tools-kimi-can-use-well" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy=""><a href="#designing-tools-kimi-can-use-well" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy="">Designing tools Kimi can use well</a></h3><p>The model only ever sees the schemas you hand it, so a tool's <strong>name and description are its entire interface</strong>. Name functions like API endpoints, where <code class="component-Code">get_service_health</code> and <code class="component-Code">search_logs</code> read as actions and <code class="component-Code">handler</code> or <code class="component-Code">doStuff</code> give the model nothing to reason about. Treat the description as <strong>instructions for choosing between tools</strong> mid-conversation, not as a changelog entry.</p><p>Constrain the arguments as tightly as the real inputs allow. An <code class="component-Code">enum</code> for a fixed set of values and <code class="component-Code">required</code> on the fields you depend on are <strong>the difference between a clean call and a malformed one</strong>. The <code class="component-Code">service</code> enum in these examples is why Kimi never invented a service name.</p><p>What a tool returns matters as much as what it accepts, because the result becomes the model's next input. <strong>Compact JSON objects with named fields</strong> like <code class="component-Code">{ "status": "degraded", "errorRate": 0.182 }</code> are easier to reason over than prose or large blobs. <strong>Keep the overall set small</strong>, too: a handful of well-scoped tools beats a large catalog, since overlapping tools force the model to guess which one you meant.</p></section>
<section id="tips"><h3 class="component-H3" id="tips" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy=""><a href="#tips" data-rw-cid-6yd66exy="">Tips</a></h3><ol class="component-OL" data-rw-cid-bls2vg63="">
<li class="component-LI" data-rw-cid-d6yeatzp="">
<p><strong>Echo every tool call back into the history.</strong> Append both the assistant message that requested the call and your <code class="component-Code">tool</code> reply. Dropping the assistant message breaks the <code class="component-Code">tool_call_id</code> pairing and the next request will error.</p>
</li>
<li class="component-LI" data-rw-cid-d6yeatzp="">
<p><strong>Parse <code class="component-Code">arguments</code> defensively.</strong> It's a JSON string the model generated. Validate it against your schema before executing, and return an error object as the tool result if it doesn't fit, so the model can correct itself on the next round.</p>
</li>
<li class="component-LI" data-rw-cid-d6yeatzp="">
<p><strong>Return errors as tool results, not exceptions.</strong> When a tool fails, send <code class="component-Code">{ "error": "..." }</code> back as the <code class="component-Code">tool</code> message. The model can read that and adjust, where a thrown exception just ends the loop.</p>
</li>
<li class="component-LI" data-rw-cid-d6yeatzp="">
<p><strong>Watch <code class="component-Code">cached_tokens</code> on long conversations.</strong> The system prompt and tool definitions repeat on every round, so Runware's prompt cache reuses them: across the incident's three rounds, <code class="component-Code">usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens</code> climbed from 0 to 608. Keeping the stable parts of your prompt in a fixed prefix maximizes that reuse.</p>
</li>
<li class="component-LI" data-rw-cid-d6yeatzp="">
<p><strong>Set the action threshold in the system prompt.</strong> Whether the model gathers context before acting, or acts immediately, is shaped by instructions like "inspect health and logs before taking action." Tighten or loosen that line to match how much autonomy you want.</p>
</li>
</ol></section></div><aside data-rw-cid-73lx6ffk=""><div class="component-Toc" data-rw-cid-gib4cwv7=""><input type="checkbox" id="toc-menu" data-rw-cid-gib4cwv7=""><div class="label"   // Seed HubSpot's consent state BEFORE its tracking code loads, and suppress
  // HubSpot's own cookie banner — our vanilla-cookieconsent banner is the sole
  // consent UI. Mirrors the main site (runware.ai) so behaviour is identical
  // across the domain. HubSpot is classified as `analytics`; it loads for
  // anonymized page views regardless, but stays cookieless until analytics
  // consent is granted (governed by `setHubSpotConsent`). HubSpot does not
  // persist this value, so the consent banner replays it on every load/change.
  ;(function () {
    window.disableHubSpotCookieBanner = true
    window._hsp = window._hsp || []

    var regionMatch = document.cookie.match(/(?:^|; )rw_consent_region=([^;]+)/)
    var region = (regionMatch) ? regionMatch[1] : 'strict_opt_in'

    var nav = navigator
    var gpc = (
      nav.globalPrivacyControl === true
      || nav.doNotTrack === '1'
      || window.doNotTrack === '1'
    )

    var functional, analytics, marketing
    var stored = null
    var storedMatch = document.cookie.match(/(?:^|; )cc_cookie=([^;]+)/)
    if (storedMatch) {
      try { stored = JSON.parse(decodeURIComponent(storedMatch[1])) } catch (e) { stored = null }
    }

    if (stored && stored.categories) {
      functional = (stored.categories.indexOf('functional') !== -1)
      analytics = (stored.categories.indexOf('analytics') !== -1)
      marketing = (stored.categories.indexOf('marketing') !== -1)
    } else {
      functional = (!gpc && region === 'optout')
      analytics = (!gpc && region === 'optout')
      marketing = (!gpc && region === 'optout')
    }

    window._hsp.push(['setHubSpotConsent', {
      analytics: analytics,
      advertisement: marketing,
      functionality: functional,
    }])
  })()
</script><script>
